summarization.a: The figure shows multiple infrared difference spectra of a B2O3 ALD film recorded after consecutive HF exposures at 150 °C. The progressive deepening of the B–O stretching band and the persistence of the B–O–B bending feature indicate rapid removal of the B2O3 film, which is nearly complete after more than five HF exposures.

data_extraction.a:
| Wavenumber region (cm⁻¹) | Vibrational assignment | Evolution with HF exposure |
|--------------------------|------------------------|----------------------------|
| ~1250–1600 | B–O stretch | Strong negative band that deepens with increasing HF exposure; nearly saturated after ~5 exposures |
| ~700–800 | B–O–B bend | Distinct negative feature that persists throughout HF cycling |
| ~1000 | Baseline region | Minor changes relative to main B–O features |

a.question: What do the changes in the B–O stretching region indicate during repeated HF exposure?
a.answer: The increasing depth of the B–O stretching band shows that B–O bonds are progressively removed as HF exposure continues. The saturation of this feature after several cycles indicates that most of the B2O3 film has been etched away.

a.question: After approximately how many HF exposures does the spectral change begin to saturate?
Which vibrational mode is responsible for the feature near 700–800 cm⁻¹?
Do the infrared spectra suggest efficient removal of the B2O3 film at 150 °C?
